options: Modern NVMe SSDs have made random and sequential access essentially equal
Random access is still meaningfully slower than sequential, even on SSDs
Sequential access on SSDs is actually slower than sequential on HDDs
On SSDs the access pattern only matters for writes, not reads
why: HI flags as a misconception that SSDs equalize access; random is still meaningfully slower than sequential (far worse on HDDs), making proper indexing critical.

options: Writes are blocked entirely while any index on the table exists
A single write may trigger several disk writes, since every index must also be updated
Indexes cost extra disk space but add no write-time overhead
Writes speed up because the index already holds a copy of the row
why: HI: on insert/update the DB updates the main table plus every index, so multiple indexes mean a single write triggers several disk writes, plus extra disk space.

options: About 5x faster, roughly 10ms down to 2ms
About 50x faster, roughly 50ms down to 1ms
About 2x faster, roughly 4ms down to 2ms
About 500x faster, roughly 50ms down to 0.1ms
why: HI: Postgres ~50ms vs Redis ~1ms, a 50x improvement, because memory sits closer to the CPU and avoids disk.

options: Writes get slow because both the cache and the DB must confirm each write
If the cache crashes before flushing to the DB, recent writes can be lost
The cache fills with entries that are written but never actually read
Each read must first consult a directory to locate the data
why: Write-behind writes only to cache and flushes to the DB asynchronously, giving fast writes but data loss if the cache crashes before flushing. Suits workloads tolerant of occasional loss.

options: Only when reads are deduplicated via request coalescing
Only when expiration is TTL-based, not invalidation triggered on writes
Only when the cache runs in-process within the application
Only when hot keys are replicated across multiple cache nodes
why: HI: warming refreshes popular keys before they expire, which only helps with TTL-based expiration. If you invalidate on writes instead, warming does not prevent stampedes.

options: Raise the TTL to several hours so entries expire far less often
Request coalescing or single-flight, where one request rebuilds while others wait
Switch to a write-through cache so entries are refreshed on every write
Shard the cache across more nodes to spread the load on a miss
why: HI calls request coalescing (single-flight) the most effective fix: one request rebuilds the entry while the rest wait, instead of hundreds hitting the DB.
accept: Raising the TTL reduces stampede frequency but does not prevent the thundering herd when an entry finally expires, so coalescing is the better answer.

options: Cache-aside is the sensible interview default for most reads
Redis supports write-through natively, no application code required
CDNs are essentially a form of read-through cache
In-process caching is an optimization layer added after an external cache
why: HI: cache-aside is the default, CDNs are read-through, and in-process caching comes after an external cache. It explicitly says Redis does NOT natively support write-through; application or framework code is needed.

options: Around 256 GiB
Around 2.5 TB
Around 256 TiB
Around 16 TB
why: HI: even Aurora maxes out around 256 TiB, the kind of ceiling that forces splitting data across machines.

options: Partitioning splits data across many machines while sharding stays on one instance
Partitioning splits data within a single DB instance while sharding splits across machines
They mean exactly the same thing with no practical distinction
Partitioning applies only to NoSQL stores while sharding applies only to SQL
why: HI: partitioning usually means splitting within one instance (ranges or hash); sharding means across machines. Terms are used loosely; what matters is one machine vs many.

options: Boolean columns cannot be fed into a hash function reliably
Two possible values cap you at two shards with badly uneven load
Each request would require an extra directory lookup to route it
The value flips when a user upgrades, forcing constant resharding
why: HI's canonical bad shard key: low cardinality (two values) caps you at two shards, and if most users are free, one shard is overloaded. Good keys need high cardinality plus even distribution.

options: Range-based sharding on a natural ordering key
Hash-based sharding on the partition key
Directory-based sharding via a lookup service
Vertical partitioning by splitting columns across tables
why: HI labels hash-based sharding the default and most common; it is what your interviewer assumes unless you explicitly state otherwise.

options: Isolate a hot key onto its own dedicated shard
Use compound shard keys such as hash of user_id plus date
Route every cross-shard write through two-phase commit by default
Denormalize related data so it stays together on one shard
why: HI recommends isolating hot keys, compound keys, and denormalizing. It warns AGAINST 2PC as the go-to (slow, fragile); prefer sagas or avoiding cross-shard transactions.

options: It spreads hot keys so popular items never overload one node
It minimizes how much data must be moved when a node is added or removed
It removes the need for a hash function by mapping keys directly
It provides strong consistency across all replicas of a key
why: HI: modulo hashing (going from %3 to %4) remaps almost every key; consistent hashing moves only a bounded fraction when membership changes.

options: Compute hash of the event ID modulo the current node count
Hash the event ID to a point on the ring, then move clockwise to the next node
Look the event ID up in a directory table that maps it to a node
Assign the event to whichever node currently reports the lowest load
why: HI: place nodes on the ring, hash the event ID to a point, then move clockwise to the next node, avoiding modulo and limiting movement on membership change.

options: Hot keys created by a single piece of extremely popular content
A failed node dumping its entire load onto its one clockwise neighbor
The need to replicate each key for durability and availability
Extending the ring beyond the 2^32 address space when nodes grow
why: HI: without virtual nodes a failed node's load shifts entirely to one neighbor (doubling it). Virtual nodes place each DB at many ring points so failure load spreads evenly.

options: Virtual nodes fix structural imbalance while replication or key-salting fix workload imbalance
Redis Cluster implements consistent hashing using virtual nodes per master
Redis Cluster uses fixed hash slots, 16,384 of them via CRC16(key) mod 16384
DynamoDB replicates each partition across 3 AZs, so a primary failure promotes a replica with no data movement
why: HI: virtual nodes fix key-distribution imbalance while replication or salting fix traffic imbalance; Redis Cluster uses fixed slots (NOT consistent hashing); DynamoDB replicates partitions across 3 AZs so failover moves no data.

options: Whether to build on a SQL or a NoSQL datastore
Whether to favor consistency or availability when a network partition occurs
Whether to scale by sharding the data or by replicating it
Whether to guarantee strong or eventual durability of writes
why: HI: partition tolerance is non-negotiable in any distributed system, so CAP boils down to one choice: prioritize consistency or availability when a partition occurs.

options: Consistency, because showing a stale display name is unacceptable
Availability, because showing the old name beats showing an error
Consistency, because profile fields are treated as financial data
Availability, but only by rejecting writes while still serving reads
why: HI: for the profile example the answer is clear, show the stale old name (availability) rather than an error, since a stale name beats no name.

options: Ticket and seat booking, to prevent double-booking the same seat
Viewing a Yelp restaurant's posted opening hours
E-commerce inventory counts, to prevent overselling stock
Stock trading order books matching buyers and sellers
why: HI lists ticket booking, inventory, and financial/trading as consistency-required. Yelp showing slightly outdated hours is an availability example; brief staleness is fine.

options: Causal consistency, where related operations are seen in order
Strong consistency, where every reader sees the latest write at once
Read-your-own-writes, where you see your updates but others may lag
Eventual consistency, where all replicas converge given enough time
why: HI: read-your-own-writes is common in social media so users see their own profile edits right away, even if others briefly see stale data.

options: Strong consistency, where reads always reflect the newest write
Causal consistency, preserving the order of related operations
Eventual consistency, where replicas converge over time
Read-your-own-writes, guaranteeing you see your own updates
why: HI: eventual consistency is the most relaxed model and the default of most distributed databases, what you implicitly choose when prioritizing availability.

options: Being unable to state the CAP theorem when asked
Doing scale math with outdated hardware numbers that underestimate modern systems
Never bringing up sharding as a scaling option
Reaching for Redis whenever caching comes up
why: HI: relying on outdated hardware constraints leads to over-engineered designs, one of the biggest tells of book-only knowledge.

options: Around 512 GiB of RAM
Around 4 TB of RAM
Around 24 TB of RAM
Around 60 TB of RAM
why: HI cites U-24tb1.metal at 24 TB RAM (X1e.32xlarge at 4 TB, M6i.32xlarge at 512 GiB); many apps that once needed distributed systems now fit on one machine.

options: Under 1ms
Roughly 1-2ms
Roughly 50-150ms
Roughly 250-300ms
why: HI: under 1ms within a single AZ, 1-2ms across AZs in a region, and 50-150ms cross-region.

options: A hard ceiling of roughly 60 TB per bucket
Effectively unlimited, with petabyte-scale considered standard practice
Capped near 256 TiB, the same ceiling as Amazon Aurora
Suitable only for cold, rarely accessed archival data
why: HI: S3 is effectively unlimited (petabyte-scale is standard); the days of storage being a primary constraint are largely behind us.

options: i3en.24xlarge offers roughly 60 TB of local SSD
D3en.12xlarge offers roughly 336 TB of HDD
Within a datacenter, 25 Gbps networking is common
A single Postgres instance should be sharded once it reaches 100GB
why: HI cites i3en.24xlarge at 60 TB SSD, D3en.12xlarge at 336 TB HDD, and 25 Gbps datacenter networking. It explicitly criticizes the textbook split-at-100GB rule as obsolete.

options: Durable streaming of messages between decoupled services
Full-text search and querying over large volumes of data
Strongly consistent transactional writes across records
Distributed locking and coordination among nodes
why: HI frames Elasticsearch around search and querying over big data via inverted indexes, not as a queue, lock service, or transactional store.

options: An index is one Lucene file that every node opens and shares
An index is split into shards, and each shard is a self-contained Lucene index
Shards are read replicas pointing at a single master Lucene instance
Index and shard are two interchangeable names for the same unit
why: HI: an index is partitioned into shards for horizontal scaling, and each shard is itself a complete Lucene index that can live on a different node.

options: Primary shard count is fixed at creation; changing it means reindexing into a new index
Adding primary shards later triggers an automatic rebalance at no real cost
Primary shard count influences only read latency, never write throughput
Elasticsearch sets the shard count automatically and it cannot be configured
why: HI: primary shard count is fixed at index creation; changing it requires reindexing into a new index, a common gotcha interviewers probe.

options: As a search layer running alongside a separate primary database
As the single primary datastore and source of truth for the system
As a tool for autocomplete, fuzzy matching, and relevance ranking
As a store backed by inverted indexes kept in sync from the DB
why: HI: Elasticsearch should generally not be your source of truth; keep a separate primary DB and sync ES as a secondary search index.

options: Change Data Capture streaming DB changes into Elasticsearch
Application-level dual writes that update both stores together
A periodic full table scan used as the only real-time sync mechanism
Clients writing search documents straight to ES, bypassing the DB
why: HI presents CDC and dual writes as the standard sync strategies. A periodic scan is not real-time sync, and bypassing the primary DB contradicts the source-of-truth pattern.

options: A B-tree keyed on document ID for fast point lookups
An inverted index mapping each term to the documents containing it
A consistent hash ring spreading documents across nodes
A skip list ordered by document timestamp for range scans
why: HI: the inverted index maps each term to the documents containing it, enabling fast full-text search, unlike a B-tree keyed on IDs.

options: A topic is the physical storage while a partition is just a logical label
A topic is a logical grouping while a partition is the physical ordered append-only log
Topic and partition describe the same underlying unit of storage
A single partition can span and serve several different topics
why: HI: a topic is a logical grouping of messages; a partition is the physical, ordered, immutable append-only log. A topic can have many partitions across brokers.

options: Every consumer reads every partition and deduplicates the overlap
Each partition is assigned to exactly one consumer within the group
Kafka load-balances each individual message randomly across consumers
The producer decides which consumer in the group handles each message
why: HI: within a consumer group each partition is assigned to exactly one consumer, so under normal operation each event goes to a single consumer.

options: Around 100GB of data and 100K messages per second
Around 1TB of data and roughly 1M messages per second
Around 10TB of data and 10M messages per second
Around 500GB of data and 50K messages per second
why: HI's hand-wavy but useful estimate: a single broker stores ~1TB and handles up to ~1M messages per second, used to decide whether scaling is even needed.

options: partition equals hash(key) mod num_partitions using the murmur2 hash
Round-robin across partitions, ignoring the message key entirely
By the message timestamp taken modulo the partition count
By routing to whichever broker currently reports the lowest load
why: HI: partition equals hash(key) mod num_partitions with murmur2 by default. A bad key causes hot partitions.

options: Random salting appended to the partition key to spread load
A compound key such as ad ID combined with region
Storing the full event payload blob directly inside Kafka
Applying back pressure to slow the producer down
why: HI lists no-key default partitioning, random salting, compound keys, and back pressure. Storing large blobs in Kafka is an anti-pattern, not a fix.

options: Strict message ordering within a single partition
Built-in consumer retries with automatic dead-letter queues
Durable replication of messages across multiple brokers
Per-consumer offset tracking to resume from a position
why: HI: Kafka has no out-of-the-box consumer retry support; you build your own retry topic plus DLQ. That is why the Web Crawler breakdown chose SQS, which provides them natively.

options: Authenticating and authorizing each incoming request
Routing each request to the appropriate backend service
Caching responses to reduce backend load
Terminating SSL and managing certificates
why: HI: candidates emphasize middleware but forget the core reason, request routing, deciding which backend handles each incoming request.

options: After routing, once the backend has accepted the request
Up front, as the first step before routing or other work
Only after a cached response has been checked or stored
Never at the gateway; validation is left to each backend
why: HI lists validation first, so malformed requests (bad JSON, missing API key) are rejected early without wasting backend resources.

options: Client-to-gateway uses a dedicated LB like ELB or NGINX; gateway-to-service is done by the gateway
The gateway handles client-to-gateway balancing internally, no separate LB needed
Load balancing is generally unrelated to API gateways in practice
Backend services balance among themselves; the gateway never load-balances
why: HI separates client-to-gateway balancing (a dedicated LB such as ELB or NGINX in front) from gateway-to-service balancing (the gateway distributing across backend instances).

options: Highly user-specific responses, to personalize output per user
Frequently accessed data that changes rarely and is not user specific
Essentially every response, since gateway caching is always safe
Only error responses, to avoid re-running failed backend calls
why: HI: gateway caching fits frequently accessed, infrequently changing, non-user-specific data where the same input returns the same result.

options: Always, even for a simple single-client client-server app
For microservices; skip it for a simple monolithic or single-client design
Only once you also need a dedicated database caching layer
Only for very small systems with fewer than three services
why: HI: use a gateway with microservices, but it is overkill for simple monolithic or single-client systems.

options: Walk through its full middleware pipeline in detail
Briefly note it handles routing plus basic middleware, then move on
Replace it conceptually with a message queue for decoupling
Model it as the system's source of truth for request state
why: HI stresses the gateway is thin and not the interesting part; the bigger risk is over-spending time, so note routing plus middleware and move on.
